days.label: Day 1
days.title: Three Classes, One Map
days.subtitle: Course codes, room numbers, and walking directions all hit at once.

days.label: Day 2
days.title: Academic Advising
days.subtitle: This is where MyASU and DARS stop sounding like secret terms.

days.label: Day 3
days.title: Assignments Start Moving
days.subtitle: Canvas notifications, syllabi, and your first office-hours email show up fast.

days.label: Day 4
days.title: Office Hours
days.subtitle: A short email becomes a real conversation with a professor.

days.label: Day 5
days.title: Priority Tasks
days.subtitle: No classes today. This is the day to clean up the small admin things before they grow teeth.

badges.title: Classroom Navigator
badges.description: You made it through three different classrooms and the schedule feels less abstract than it did this morning.

badges.title: Schedule Decoder
badges.description: You used MyASU to match times, buildings, and the next day instead of hoping memory would do the work.

badges.title: Advising Check-In
badges.description: You saw what academic advising actually looks like and how DARS and MyASU can get translated into plain language.

badges.title: Syllabus Scanner
badges.description: You checked the course pages early enough to see deadlines before they turned into surprises.

badges.title: Office Hours Drafted
badges.description: You learned that a short honest email is enough to ask for help before confusion gets bigger.

badges.title: Office Hours Visited
badges.description: You saw that office hours are usually a quiet conversation, not a performance.

badges.title: Priority Tasks Handled
badges.description: You opened MyASU and handled the admin tasks that can quietly trip up a first week.

endings.title: You Feel Ready
endings.summary: The week was still a lot. But now the buildings, systems, and people at ASU feel less like a code you were supposed to already know.

endings.title: You're Finding Your Rhythm
endings.summary: Some parts of campus are starting to make sense. The rest will come faster now that you know where to look.

endings.title: You're Still Adjusting
endings.summary: The week felt heavy and unclear. That does not mean you are behind. It means this is new, and the support is still here when you want it.

jumpIns.title: First-Day Class Tour
jumpIns.recap: You are about to move through Intro to Python, Math, and Chemistry on the Tempe campus for the first time.

jumpIns.title: Advising Jump-In
jumpIns.recap: You already survived the first day of classes. Now you are heading into academic advising with real questions about MyASU and DARS.

jumpIns.title: Office Hours Jump-In
jumpIns.recap: You sent the email the night before. Now you are walking toward office hours with your notes and one real question.

scenes.title: Your Monday starts with a map and a stomach drop.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: It is your first week at ASU, and nobody in your family ever handed down a decoder for course codes, room numbers, or which hallways matter. Today you have three classes: Intro to Python, Math, and Chemistry.

scenes.title: First stop: Intro to Python.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: This room is bigger than you expected. Laptops are already open, the syllabus is on the screen, and everyone else somehow looks like they knew what COOR 174 meant before today.
routeSteps: From Palm Walk, head west on Orange Mall toward the Engineering Center.
Look for Coor Hall just before the main engineering courtyards.
Room 174 is on the first floor near the large lecture entrances.

cards.title: What the room feels like
cards.body: A large lecture room with rows of seats, bright screens, and a professor moving quickly through the course structure.

cards.title: What you actually do
cards.body: Match the course number, choose an open seat, and let yourself settle before deciding you are behind.

cards.title: First-gen reality check
cards.body: Not knowing building codes on sight is normal. Most students learn them by repetition, not by magic.

bullets: Screenshot the room number in MyASU if codes like COOR 174 still feel abstract.
Look for the course number on the slide before assuming you are in the wrong place.
The first class is mostly orientation. You are allowed to just take it in.
scenes.videoCaption: Use this short preview to picture the pace, seating, and lecture-room vibe before you walk in.

scenes.title: Math is smaller and quieter.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: The math room is not as big as Python. There is a whiteboard across the front, a tighter seating layout, and more room for people to actually notice each other.
routeSteps: Leave Coor Hall and walk east back along Orange Mall toward Palm Walk.
Pass Hayden Library and keep Payne Hall on your right.
Room 153 sits in the ground-floor classroom wing, just past the lobby.

cards.title: What the room feels like
cards.body: A medium classroom with more eye contact, more board work, and a pace that makes it easier to ask where you are.

cards.title: What helps here
cards.body: Sit where you can see the board clearly and write down every time the professor mentions due dates or office hours.

cards.title: Why this matters
cards.body: Different classes feel different. Learning the room style is part of learning the course.

bullets: Smaller rooms often feel more personal, not more dangerous.
If you miss one instruction, you can still catch it later in Canvas or the syllabus.
You do not need to look completely settled to belong there.
scenes.videoCaption: This preview is here so the layout and pace feel familiar before you take your seat.

scenes.title: Chemistry feels formal, but not impossible.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: By your third class, you are tired enough to stop pretending this is effortless. The chemistry room has the feel of a real science course: slides, safety reminders, and a lot of new information packed into a normal hour.
routeSteps: From Payne Hall, cross toward Cady Mall and keep moving north into the science buildings.
Look for Life Sciences A beside the inner science courtyards.
Room 104 is near the front lobby on the first floor.

cards.title: What the room feels like
cards.body: A lecture room with science-course energy: slides moving quickly, reminders about lab expectations, and students trying to capture everything.

cards.title: What to focus on
cards.body: Course structure, lab expectations, and where assignments will actually show up after class.

cards.title: What you just proved
cards.body: You can move between different buildings, room styles, and course vibes without already knowing the campus by heart.

bullets: Three classes in one day is enough to make anyone feel scrambled.
Getting there counts, even if you still need to look at your phone before every turn.
The campus will feel smaller by repetition, not by force.
scenes.videoCaption: Watch this before class if science-building rooms still feel hard to picture.

scenes.title: That night, what do you do with tomorrow's advising appointment?
scenes.speaker: You
scenes.body: You can either open MyASU now and screenshot the advising details, or tell yourself you will remember one more new system in the morning.

choices.label: "Open MyASU now and save the details."
choices.caption: Use the calm ten minutes you still have.

choices.label: "I'll remember it tomorrow."
choices.caption: Push the admin stuff off for now.

scenes.title: You make tomorrow easier on purpose.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: You screenshot the appointment time, the building, and the room details. The system feels less hostile when you look at it before the rush starts.

scenes.title: The details stay fuzzy.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Nothing catastrophic happens. But tomorrow still feels blurry because the appointment lives somewhere in MyASU that you have not opened yet.

scenes.title: Day 2 starts with academic advising.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: This is one of those ASU things people mention like you should already know what it looks like. In reality, it is a front desk, a waiting area, and a chance to ask the questions you do not want to keep carrying alone.

scenes.title: Before you check in, what do you do?
scenes.speaker: You
scenes.body: Your phone is full of notes, but your brain is louder than the screen.

choices.label: "Write down two questions first."
choices.caption: Give the meeting something concrete to start with.

choices.label: "Just walk in and hope the conversation carries me."
choices.caption: Rely on the advisor to pull the questions out.

scenes.title: You walk in with real questions, which helps.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: The front desk checks you in, the waiting room is quieter than you feared, and the advisor starts with the exact questions you wrote down: what DARS shows, what to watch in MyASU, and who to contact when something looks off.
routeSteps: Enter the Student Services Building and look for advising signage near the front lobby.
Check in with your name or ASURITE, then wait for your advisor to call you back.
Bring screenshots of your schedule, MyASU tasks, and anything in DARS that feels confusing.

cards.title: What the space looks like
cards.body: A normal office waiting area: front desk, chairs, a few students, low voices, not a test.

cards.title: What comes up
cards.body: DARS, your class plan, MyASU holds, and the questions that feel too small to email to five different offices.

cards.title: What to ask
cards.body: You can ask what DARS means, whether your classes make sense together, and what office handles the next problem.

bullets: Being first-gen does not mean you are behind. It means the hidden steps are actually hidden.
Bring screenshots if you do not know the exact ASU terms yet.
Advising is allowed to be practical.
scenes.videoCaption: This preview shows the kind of front desk, waiting area, and conversation setup you can expect.

scenes.title: You still get help, even without perfect questions.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: The advisor still helps, but the first few minutes go to translating a stressed feeling into a useful question. It is still worth going. It just takes longer to get specific.
routeSteps: Enter the Student Services Building and follow the advising signs near the front lobby.
Check in at the desk even if you still feel unsure what to ask.
Use the conversation to say what feels confusing in plain language first.

cards.title: What the space looks like
cards.body: A regular office waiting area with students doing exactly what you are doing: trying to make the system less confusing.

cards.title: What helps mid-meeting
cards.body: Once you say, 'I do not really know what DARS is showing me,' the advisor has something concrete to respond to.

cards.title: What you learn anyway
cards.body: You do not need polished language to start a useful advising conversation.

bullets: It is okay if the first question sounds rough.
Advisors are used to translating systems language into something students can actually use.
Showing up counts.
scenes.videoCaption: Even if you arrive unsure, this preview helps you picture the room before you walk in.

scenes.title: By day 3, the campus looks slightly less impossible.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: You still check the building signs twice, but now Intro to Python, Math, and Chemistry feel like real places instead of random codes. The next wave is different: Canvas notifications, syllabi, and small deadlines.

scenes.title: After classes, what do you do first?
scenes.speaker: You
scenes.body: Three courses means three syllabi, three Canvas pages, and three different ways professors say 'this is important.'

scenes.title: Tomorrow gets louder fast.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: You wake up to more notifications than expected and have to sort deadlines while already moving between classes. Avoiding the syllabus does not make it less real.

scenes.title: Opening Canvas early makes the week easier to read.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: You open each course page before the noise stacks up. Now you know where the Python modules live, what math expects each week, and which chemistry items are tied to lab and lecture.

cards.title: Intro to Python
cards.body: Find the course modules, the first assignment window, and where announcements will show up.

cards.title: Math
cards.body: Look for homework systems, office-hours times, and any line about how questions should be asked.

cards.title: Chemistry
cards.body: Check lecture expectations, lab reminders, and anything labeled safety, attendance, or quiz.

bullets: You are not supposed to memorize three syllabi at once.
The goal is to see where information lives before you urgently need it.
One calm scan now saves panic later.

scenes.title: Even a late check still helps.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Once you finally open the course pages, the confusion becomes more concrete. It is not fun, but it is better than carrying three unnamed worries at once.

cards.title: What to look for first
cards.body: Assignments due this week, office-hours info, and any note that says where updates will appear.

cards.title: What this changes
cards.body: The work is still there, but at least it has names, due dates, and places to click.

bullets: Late is still better than never.
Confusion shrinks once it has labels.
Tomorrow's version of you will be glad this is no longer invisible.

scenes.title: You want to go to office hours tomorrow. What happens tonight?
scenes.speaker: You
scenes.body: The professor said office hours are open, but the email still feels harder to send than it should.

choices.label: "Send the short honest email now."
choices.caption: You do not need perfect wording.

choices.label: "Keep rewriting it until it feels embarrassing."
choices.caption: Overthinking is still a form of delay.

scenes.title: The email gets longer while your confidence gets smaller.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: You keep editing the same three sentences like the wrong wording might prove you do not belong here. It never becomes perfect. It just becomes later.

scenes.title: A short email is enough.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: You send a plain, honest email asking about tomorrow's office hours and what to bring. The professor replies like this is normal, because it is.

emailMock.subject: Question about office hours for MAT 170
emailMock.body: Hi Professor, I am in your MAT 170 class and I want to come to office hours tomorrow because I am still confused about some of the first homework steps. Is it okay if I bring my notes and ask a few questions? Thank you.
emailMock.replySubject: Re: Question about office hours for MAT 170
emailMock.replyBody: Hi, yes, absolutely. Bring your notes or the homework page and come by during office hours tomorrow in PSH 242. We can walk through where you are getting stuck.

cards.title: What to say
cards.body: Course name, why you are coming, and what you plan to bring are enough.

cards.title: What the reply tells you
cards.body: Office hours are for exactly this: confusion early, before it compounds.

bullets: You do not need a polished academic email voice.
Short and honest beats perfect and delayed.
The room number in the reply matters tomorrow.

scenes.title: You send it anyway, just later and shakier.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: The email still goes out. The professor still answers kindly. The important part is that you now have a time, a room number, and permission to show up without pretending you already understand everything.

emailMock.subject: MAT 170 office hours question
emailMock.body: Hi Professor, I am sorry if this is obvious, but I am still confused about part of the homework and wanted to ask if I could come to office hours tomorrow. Thank you.
emailMock.replyBody: Hi, yes, please come by tomorrow in PSH 242 and bring the homework page or your notes. That is what office hours are for.

bullets: A slightly awkward email still works.
You do not lose the right to ask because you felt nervous first.
Now you know where to go tomorrow.

scenes.title: Office hours are quieter than the phrase makes them sound.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: You know the office location because the reply email spelled it out. The hard part now is walking through the door instead of circling the hallway.

scenes.title: What do you do when the professor says, 'Come in'?
scenes.speaker: You
scenes.body: This is the part people skip when they only say 'go to office hours' and forget how new it can feel.

choices.label: "Show your notes and ask about the first problem."
choices.caption: Let the conversation start with something real.

choices.label: "Hover for a minute and hope you do not sound lost."
choices.caption: Try to hide the nerves first.

scenes.title: You stall in the hallway for a minute.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: You almost convince yourself to leave. Then you remember the professor already answered your email and literally expects students to show up here.

scenes.title: This is what office hours actually feels like.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: It is one desk, a couple of chairs, your notes, and a professor who is glad you showed up before the confusion got bigger.
routeSteps: Use the room number from the reply email and head into the second-floor office hallway in Payne Hall.
Look for the office placard beside the door before you knock.
Bring the problem set, notes, or screenshot you want to ask about.

emailMock.subject: Question about office hours for MAT 170
emailMock.body: Hi Professor, I am in your MAT 170 class and I want to come to office hours tomorrow because I am still confused about some of the first homework steps. Is it okay if I bring my notes and ask a few questions? Thank you.
emailMock.replySubject: Re: Question about office hours for MAT 170
emailMock.replyBody: Hi, yes, absolutely. Bring your notes or the homework page and come by during office hours tomorrow in PSH 242. We can walk through where you are getting stuck.

cards.title: What the room looks like
cards.body: A normal office or office-adjacent space: desk, chairs, papers, maybe another student waiting, nothing theatrical.

cards.title: How to start talking
cards.body: Open your notes and say what part stopped making sense. That is enough to begin.

cards.title: What happens next
cards.body: The professor points to the step you missed, explains what to try next, and turns a vague panic into a smaller problem.

bullets: Office hours are for clarification, not just emergencies.
Bringing one real question is better than waiting to feel perfectly prepared.
The conversation is usually shorter and calmer than the fear around it.
scenes.videoCaption: This is the kind of quiet office setup and conversation pace you can expect on a first visit.

scenes.title: Even an awkward start becomes a real conversation.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Your first sentence comes out rough. Then the professor looks at your notes, asks one clarifying question, and the room stops feeling so intimidating.
routeSteps: Use the room number from the reply email and head into the second-floor office hallway in Payne Hall.
Knock, step in, and let the notes on your page do some of the talking for you.
You do not need a perfect explanation before the conversation starts.

cards.title: What the room looks like
cards.body: A small office conversation, not a performance stage.

cards.title: What matters most
cards.body: You still showed up, which gives the professor something real to respond to.

scenes.videoCaption: This preview is here to make the room and conversation format feel familiar before you go.

scenes.title: No classes today. That is exactly why this matters.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Free days disappear quickly. This is the moment to clear the first-week admin tasks that can quietly block the rest of the semester if you leave them vague.

scenes.title: What do you do with the quiet morning?
scenes.speaker: You
scenes.body: Admin screens are rarely fun. They are still easier now than they will be when a deadline is attached.

choices.label: "Open MyASU and knock out the priority tasks now."
choices.caption: Do the small annoying things while they are still small.

choices.label: "Avoid it a little longer because the screen is confusing."
choices.caption: Keep the stress in the background for now.

scenes.title: The uncertainty stays open in the background.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: When you avoid MyASU tasks, they do not stop existing. They just sit there waiting until the timing is worse.

scenes.title: Small admin tasks feel smaller once they are visible.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: You open MyASU, not because you suddenly love paperwork, but because it is easier to handle one task now than five problems later.

cards.title: Why this helps
cards.body: Priority tasks, holds, and missing forms feel more manageable once they are named and sorted.

cards.title: What to keep nearby
cards.body: Your student ID, any requested document names, and a place to write down what still needs follow-up.

taskChecklist.title: Check Priority Tasks
taskChecklist.detail: Open the MyASU task list and note anything marked urgent or time-sensitive.

taskChecklist.title: Submit missing documents
taskChecklist.detail: Upload or gather anything tied to residency, financial aid, immunization, or identity verification.

taskChecklist.title: Review holds and alerts
taskChecklist.detail: See whether anything in MyASU could affect registration, billing, or access later.

taskChecklist.title: Add due dates to your calendar
taskChecklist.detail: Pull the first deadlines from Python, Math, and Chemistry into one place you will actually check.

taskChecklist.title: Look at next week's schedule once
taskChecklist.detail: Use the calm day to remind yourself which buildings and rooms are coming next.

bullets: Admin work feels academic the moment it starts affecting classes.
You do not have to finish the whole semester today. You just need to make the next week legible.
The point is fewer surprises, not perfect control.

scenes.title: Even a partial check helps.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: You finally open MyASU and realize the screen is annoying, not impossible. A few tasks still need attention, but now you at least know what is waiting.

taskChecklist.title: See the urgent items first
taskChecklist.detail: Start with anything the dashboard marks as time-sensitive.

taskChecklist.title: Write down one next action
taskChecklist.detail: If you cannot solve it today, at least leave yourself a plain-language note about what to do next.

taskChecklist.title: Look at next week's class locations
taskChecklist.detail: Even one glance at the building names makes Monday easier.

bullets: Partial progress is still progress.
The screen gets less intimidating once it has been opened once.
You can come back to the rest with more context.

scenes.title: Week one did not get smaller. You got more context.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: The buildings are still big. The systems are still new. But depending on the choices you made, ASU now feels either a little more legible or still mostly opaque.

scenes.title: End of week one
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Your week-one result depends on how often you checked, asked, and showed up before confusion had to do all the talking.
